problem_summary: 2015 MCM Problem C (ICM, interdisciplinary): model "organizational churn" in a 370-person company (ICM) given its org chart and HR data. The team must capture how resignations diffuse through the organization, model the HR manager's reactions (promotion + recruitment) to fill vacancies, estimate productivity and cost, test sustainability under higher churn rates, and recommend strategies. This O-award paper ("Organizational Churn: A Roll of the Dice?", control #37075) reframes the whole problem as network science: it builds a "Human Capital network" over the 370 positions and runs an agent-based monthly simulation in which each employee's leave/stay decision is a Bayesian-updated Beta-Bernoulli draw that spreads churn information across the network.
models_used: Human Capital network: a graph G=(V,E) over all 370 positions, with edges for same-office, office-head-to-related-upper-office-head, and senior-manager-to-senior-manager links; used as the substrate for information diffusion

Bayesian-learning churn model: each individual i draws a leave/stay indicator u_i^(t) via p_i ~ Beta(alpha_i, beta_i) then u_i ~ Bernoulli(p_i) — a Beta-Bernoulli / Beta-Binomial conjugate model, with leave-probability = beta/(alpha+beta)

Conjugate Bayesian hyperparameter updating: each month alpha increases on observed 'stay' signals and beta on observed 'leave' signals from neighbors, with normalization so alpha+beta grows by 1 per month (mimics Beta posterior convergence to a Dirac delta / true p)
Network distance-weighted information reduction: churn signals from a neighbor at graph distance d are attenuated by 1/d (text also states reducing by d^2), so nearby resignations matter more

Three promotion strategies (decision rules): Experience-Oriented (longest tenure), Dissatisfaction/Likelihood-Oriented (highest beta/alpha churn risk), Centrality-Oriented (highest closeness centrality)
Recruitment model: capped HR effort (~9% of 370), priority queue filling highest-level vacancies first, fixed renewal period (e.g. 6 months) — knobs are effort cap and renewal frequency

Closeness-centrality-weighted organizational productivity metric P^(t) = sum of w_i * p_i, where individual productivity combines salary level S, training investment T*t, and a dissatisfaction discount factor 1/(1+alpha*ln(1+sigma))
Dissatisfaction model sigma_i^(t): exponentially-decaying (memory-decay) sum over past resignations of neighbors weighted by 1/d_ij^2 (inverse-square network distance)
Direct vs Indirect effect decomposition of a resignation's productivity loss (DE = lost productivity of leaver; IE = dissatisfaction-induced productivity drop in remaining staff)
Monte Carlo agent-based simulation: 50 runs averaged, monthly time steps, projected over 5 and 20 years

Extension models (qualitative): team-science shared-cognition measure sum w_i * t^2(i,j) over co-working time, team-training multiplier, and multilayer-network coupling (friendship/trust/teammate layers with cross-layer coupling edges)
key_techniques: Reframing an HR problem as a network-science + Bayesian-inference problem — the central conceptual move that unifies churn, promotion, and productivity on one graph

Using Beta-Bernoulli conjugacy so that 'churn risk' (beta/alpha), 'confidence/seniority' (alpha+beta controlling variance), and 'social contagion' (neighbor-driven updates) all fall out of two interpretable hyperparameters

Calibrating the prior to the data: setting beta/(alpha+beta)=p/12 to hit a target annual churn rate, and assigning larger alpha+beta (lower variance) to higher-level positions to encode that senior decisions are less volatile (alpha,beta = 144,120,64,48,32,24,24 by level)
Encoding social distance via shortest-path graph distance with inverse-square attenuation (1/d^2) for both churn contagion and dissatisfaction

Reproducing stylized facts of the real company as validation: ~18% overall churn, ~30% for middle managers vs ~15% elsewhere, and a steadily rising churn trend (18%->20% over 5 yrs) — emergent from the model, not hard-coded
Decomposing resignation cost into direct + indirect effects to quantify contagion (indirect > direct with a stable ratio)
Turning the model into actionable HR levers: tuning recruitment renewal interval (6->4->3 months) and effort cap to sustain 80% staffing under 25%/35% churn
Counter-intuitive finding surfaced via 'pure promotion, no recruitment' visualization: low tiers collapse while top tiers survive (promotion sustains the top, recruitment sustains the bottom)
Strategy comparison table showing the Likelihood (churn-risk) promotion rule beats Experience by +2.6% headcount and +11% productivity at year 10

Sensitivity analysis isolating the dissatisfaction parameter alpha: productivity robust (<5% change for 50% alpha change) but the indirect/direct ratio is sensitive, with a psychological-realism justification
why_it_won: "Judge view: it wins because it imposes a single elegant, defensible mathematical lens (network + conjugate Bayesian learning) on a fuzzy interdisciplinary HR prompt, and that lens does real work — every sub-question (contagion, promotion, productivity, cost, sustainability, strategy) is answered by the same machinery rather than bolted-on heuristics. The Beta-Bernoulli choice is sophisticated yet self-justifying: two parameters simultaneously encode churn probability, seniority-dependent volatility, and peer-influenced contagion, which reads as 'principled, not arbitrary' to a judge. It validates itself against the real company's stylized facts (18% overall, ~30% middle-manager churn, rising trend) — emergent reproduction of known behavior is exactly the credibility signal judges reward. It produces genuinely interesting, non-obvious results (higher churn -> lower productivity-cost ratio; promote-the-likely-leaver beats promote-the-senior by 11%; low tiers die first without recruitment) that translate directly into HR heuristics. And it is honest and self-aware: explicit strengths/weaknesses, a real sensitivity analysis, and a forward-looking team-science + multilayer-network extension that shows breadth without overreaching. The summary sheet is crisp and frames the whole approach in one paragraph."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet (p.1): one paragraph that names the network framing, the Beta-Binomial Bayesian churn model, the three promotion measures, and the headline counter-intuitive result (higher churn -> lower productivity-cost ratio) — a judge knows the whole thesis before page 2. Section 3.1 Constructing the Human Capital Network: transparently merges the given table + org graph into a concrete 370-position level allocation with three stated assumptions, then gives a clean formal edge definition — turns ambiguous problem data into a rigorous object. Section 4.1 Modeling Staff Churn: textbook-clean build-up from a generic Bayesian-learning primer (4.1.1) to the specific churn model (4.1.2) to a boxed algorithm (4.1.3), so the reader is never lost. The 'Determining the Prior / Updating / Information Reduction' subheadings explicitly pre-empt the three obvious objections. Section 5 Simulations is organized one-task-per-subsection (Task 1-6) mirroring the problem statement, making it trivial for a judge to check coverage. Table 2 (strategy comparison) and Figure 8 (HR-health heat visualization over 0/12/18/24 months) are the two most persuasive exhibits. Section 7 sensitivity analysis and 8 strengths/weaknesses are unusually candid (e.g. admitting simulation volatility and linear-training-cost unrealism)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Heavy reliance on Monte Carlo with high run-to-run volatility (acknowledged) — 50 runs averaged still leaves noisy boxplots; results are statistical, not crisp, raising computational cost

Internal inconsistency in the contagion attenuation: prose says reduce by d^2, but the boxed algorithm and dissatisfaction formula use 1/d (algorithm) and 1/d^2 (sigma) — the exact distance kernel is stated three slightly different ways

Many self-admittedly unrealistic assumptions: productivity rises linearly with cumulative training cost, employees have no position preferences/skill matching, nobody retires or is fired, salary level is taken as a proxy for contribution

The Beta-Bernoulli update normalization (alpha+beta grows by exactly 1/month) is a convenient modeling choice rather than derived; the mapping from neighbor signals to alpha-hat/beta-hat is somewhat ad hoc

Prior hyperparameters per level (144/120/.../24) and added simulation assumptions (experience requirements 48/24/12/0 months, 9% effort) are asserted with light justification — sensitivity is tested only for the dissatisfaction alpha, not for these

Task 6 (team science + multilayer networks) is entirely qualitative/speculative — measures are 'defined informally' and never simulated due to lack of data, so the extension is more literature-aware hand-waving than implemented model
Productivity/contribution proxied by relative salary risks circularity (it partly rewards the model for keeping highly-paid people regardless of real output)
Closeness centrality is used as both a productivity weight and a promotion criterion, which can double-count the same structural signal
reusable_patterns: Reframe a messy domain problem onto a graph: turn organizational/relational data into an explicit node-edge structure first, then run everything (diffusion, centrality weighting, distance-based influence) on that single substrate

Use a conjugate Bayesian pair (here Beta-Bernoulli/Beta-Binomial) when you need a quantity that simultaneously represents a probability, an uncertainty/confidence, AND a peer-update mechanism — interpret the hyperparameters as domain quantities
Calibrate priors directly to a target real-world statistic (set mean = known rate, set variance via total pseudo-counts to encode group-specific volatility)
Validate an emergent model by reproducing the problem's known stylized facts rather than only optimizing an objective — make the judge see the real system fall out of the model
Distance-weighted social influence: attenuate peer effects by a function of shortest-path graph distance (1/d or 1/d^2) plus exponential time/memory decay
Decompose an impact into direct + indirect (contagion) components and report their ratio as a headline insight
Structure simulations as one subsection per problem sub-question (Task 1...Task N) so coverage is auditable at a glance
Convert model parameters into actionable operational levers (recruitment renewal interval, effort cap, promotion threshold) and report the specific settings that hit a target (e.g. 80% staffing)
Run a 'remove one mechanism' experiment (here: no recruitment) to surface counter-intuitive structural insights

Always pair a quantitative sensitivity analysis on the most arbitrary parameter with an honest, itemized strengths/weaknesses section; close with a forward-looking, literature-grounded extension to demonstrate breadth
